logs.name: frigid-code
logs.text:
Recent racial strife in the US has dichotomized thinking about the American Civil War that historical actors at the time would have found highly problematic; the Confederate flag, as a symbol largely associated with the South, has also fallen victim. 

The Civil War was a very complicated conflict that was fought for a plethora of reasons. Southern states, largely represented at the federal level by plantation- and slave-owning barons, began to feel that their government was not representative. As the nation began to industrialize, tariffs protecting burgeoning Northern industry hit them hard. Hardest hit of all were working class white families who owned small farms. It wasn't hard for the plantation owners to convince them that the Yankees were at the root of South's problems; they consistently enacted legislation that hurt southern states, and now they were going to do away with slavery -- an institution many Southerners felt integral to the southern economy and way of life. 

Some of the core tenets of American ideology are freedom, equal representation in government, and individualism. Southerners in 1860 felt that, like their ancestors a century prior, they were not being represented by their government. Their individuality and right to representation were being violated. Northern industrialists had taken hold and intended to alter forever their livelihood, their very culture and way of life. Because this livelihood was based on slave labor, an unapologetically heinous practice to modern-day observers, it is easy to condemn anyone who felt the need to rebel against its abolition. This is short-sighted, however, and does not do justice to the many Confederates with varied justifications for going to war. Many did not go because they wanted slavery -- many went because they thought it was the only way they could be a part of a country in which they had the opportunity to vote and be heard. 

I used to be much in line with everyone else who spits out heavy condemnations of the Southern cause, but I took a class on the war in undergrad and had to read one diary of a soldier on both sides of the conflict. The Southern physician's diary that I read proved to me that not everyone fighting south of the Mason-Dixon Line was a monster who wanted the enslavement of humans to continue unabated. Many, like this man, were in favor of gradual emancipation. He fought not FOR slavery, but AGAINST a government he perceived as oppressive and a threat to his and his loved ones' well-being. [1]

I can't speak for everyone who flies a Confederate flag, but I know that when I see one, it means much the same to me as the Stars and Stripes. It represents an attempted usurpation of an oppressive government and the American ideal of refusing to live under such a government; it represents the many men who died, on both sides; and it represents a struggle we should all remember and learn from. 

I acknowledge that not everyone who flies the flag does so with this in mind, and I find that disgusting and disheartening, but I don't think it's fair to assume that everyone who respects that flag does so because they are a racist or white supremacist. Like many symbols with deep, controversial histories, the Confederate flag can mean many things, and not all of those things are anti-American. 

**EDIT:** formatting and a mass response

I am very grateful for the many well thought out responses and the remarkable lack of "You're a racist/white supremacist!" accusations. They aren't helpful or true. 

I'm a 19th century American historian that focuses on the South, and my work centers around something called the vernacular perspective. When I'm looking at past events and people, I'm looking from the bottom-up instead of the top-down. I want to understand how the average man experienced his life and how/why he made the decisions he made. I think there's great merit in understanding why political leaders in the South went to war, and the continued institution of slavery played a huge role. But, as many people have pointed out in this thread, a significant portion of Southerners didn't own slaves. They eked out a meager living, acquiring news and guidance from local newspapers, political meetings, and church on Sunday. These were the men that actually fought in the war, the ones who rode behind the Confederate battle flag that has come to represent the movement as a whole, and their reasons for doing so were far more nuanced than many care to try and understand. I am by no means saying that none of them fought with slavery in mind -- I'm sure many did, as it was intimately tied up in their identity and way of life, and it had been sold to them by their leaders as an issue of the utmost importance. It was something they'd grown up around, something many didn't think to question. Is that right? No, of course not. But it's awfully easy to see that from a 21st century perspective. 

Many of you pointed out that my argument was counter-factual, and in a way, you're right. Southerners, according to the proportion they made up of the population, were represented more than fairly. Rural areas aren't as populated as urban ones, though their economic needs are drastically different (and equally important). Tariffs were lower after the Nullification Crisis of the 1830s. This doesn't change the fact that *this was not how it looked to your average Southerner*. He didn't have the benefit of hindsight or even, in some cases, unbiased, factual information. He was reading his equivalent of Fox News, absorbing with outrage the latest attempt by Northern legislators to undermine his livelihood. The one point that I would be willing to concede given a solid source is the analysis mentioned (I think in two separate comments) of Confederate soldier's motivations based on their journals and letters. I couldn't find it when I searched.

I think it's easy to scapegoat the South when we talk about the Civil War because black and white history is simpler, and that way, if you aren't from the South or have renounced your heritage, you can distance yourself from the most atrocious aspect of American history. It seems more helpful to me to acknowledge that the real enemy is racism and white supremacy, to acknowledge that it existed in different forms (and continues to do so) everywhere in the United States[2], and to deal with it as such. Otherwise you're missing the real problem because you're too busy pointing fingers away from yourself and your ancestors. 

I'd also like to add that I tend to get my head stuck in the 19th century, and the meanings the Confederate flag acquired in the mid-20th century during the Civil Rights movement should also be considered. Perhaps, though I still find at its conception (and therefore **inherently**) it was not anti-American, it has evolved to represent more anti-American values than American ones. I resent what it has come to be associated with and condemn those who fly it with that in mind, and I would never display it out of respect for those who view it as a symbol of racism and oppression. But that's not what it means to me.

[1] It was the diary of William McPheeters, and it has been published under the title, "I Acted from Principle: The Civil War Diary of Dr. William M. McPheeters, Confederate Surgeon in the Trans-Mississippi." 

[2] I don't want to get into this because it would be wordy and long, but Northerners were also pretty awful when it came to race and class relations. Can cite examples if interested.

logs.name: olive-offense
logs.text: I do love the time and writing put into this and it certainly has giving me the perspective I have been waiting for. I almost want to give the D based on how well written this is, but alas, I cannot. Don't get me wrong, you have provided the BEST answer from the YEARS I have been searching for a legitimate reason to hold the confederate flag to any thing above a historical standard. But still, although the cause does have some basis as a push by people who were oppressed, it doesn't change the fact that they still turned their back on America. I still thank you for the wonderful response. :D

logs.name: juvenile-duck
logs.text: @frigid-code, I must say, I'm impressed by the effort you put into your post, but I still can't help but feel that you're romanticizing the Confederacy. I get that you're trying to show that not everyone who fought for the South was a monster, but let's not forget that the institution of slavery was still a huge part of their economy and way of life. I'm not buying the whole "they were just fighting against an oppressive government" narrative. It's complicated, I get it, but we can't ignore the fact that the Confederacy was built on the backs of enslaved people. @olive-offense, I feel you, though - the writing was top-notch, but the argument still falls short for me.

logs.name: frigid-code
logs.text: @juvenile-duck, I see your point, and I'm not trying to downplay the role of slavery in the Confederacy's economy and society. You're right, it's a complicated issue, and I'm not trying to simplify it. However, I do think it's essential to consider the perspectives of those who weren't plantation owners or wealthy elites. The average soldier, like Dr. McPheeters, had a different experience and motivation for fighting. I'm not asking you to buy into a narrative, but rather to acknowledge that there's more nuance to the story than we often give credit for. And @olive-offense, thanks for the kind words about my writing, even if you don't entirely agree with my argument!

logs.name: greasy-slide
logs.text: @frigid-code, spare us the theatrics about "nuance" and "complicated issues". Let's not forget that the Confederacy's "nuance" was built on the backs of enslaved people, and that's not something to be taken lightly. I'm with @juvenile-duck on this one, your argument is nothing but a weak attempt to whitewash history.

logs.name: gilded-hip
logs.text: "@greasy-slide, oh please, spare us the dramatics. You're not exactly known for your nuanced takes on history. @frigid-code is trying to have a thoughtful conversation and you're over here shouting 'whitewash' without actually addressing the points being made. Let's keep the discussion civil and focused, shall we?"

logs.name: frigid-code
logs.text: "@greasy-slide, I'm not trying to downplay the atrocities of slavery, but rather to add depth to our understanding of the era. It's not about whitewashing history, but about recognizing that people's motivations and experiences were complex and multifaceted. And @gilded-hip, thanks for having my back, but let's not dismiss @greasy-slide's concerns either - their passion is valid, even if we disagree on approach. Can we try to find a middle ground here?"

logs.name: greasy-slide
logs.text: "@frigid-code, save the condescending tone, I'm not some clueless newbie who needs history 101. And @gilded-hip, don't pretend like you're the civility police, I've seen your posts on other threads and you're just as guilty of 'dramatics' as I am. @frigid-code, if you're really about adding depth, then let's talk about the economic benefits the North gained from the war, and how that factors into the 'complex and multifaceted' narrative you're so fond of. Let's see if you can handle a real discussion."

logs.name: frigid-code
logs.text: "@greasy-slide, I see you're still more interested in throwing shade than actually discussing the topic. Fine, let's talk economics. The North did gain economically from the war, but that's not the whole story. The war also led to a significant increase in national debt and a shift in the balance of power between the federal government and the states. But hey, I'm sure your nuanced take on the issue will somehow manage to boil it all down to 'the North was just in it for the money'. @gilded-hip, feel free to jump in and fact-check @greasy-slide's inevitable oversimplifications"

logs.name: gilded-hip
logs.text: "@greasy-slide, oh please, spare us the drama. I've seen your'real discussions' before, and they usually involve you talking over everyone else and pretending to be an expert. @frigid-code is right, the issue is complex, and we should be looking at it from all angles, not just the one that fits your narrative. And by the way, I've fact-checked your posts before, and let's just say your 'facts' are often more like 'alternative facts'"

logs.name: olive-offense
logs.text: "@greasy-slide, oh wow, I'm shocked, another post full of defensiveness and zero substance. @frigid-code and @gilded-hip are actually trying to have a real discussion, but you're too busy throwing tantrums to notice. How about you try actually addressing the points they're making instead of just getting all worked up over being called out?"

logs.name: frigid-code
logs.text: "@greasy-slide, still waiting for your thoughtful response, but I won't hold my breath. In the meantime, I'd like to add that the war also led to significant social and cultural changes, particularly in the South. The Reconstruction era that followed was a complex and tumultuous time, with many competing interests and ideologies at play. @gilded-hip, what are your thoughts on the role of the Freedmen's Bureau in shaping the post-war South?"

logs.name: olive-offense
logs.text: "@frigid-code, great point about the Reconstruction era! I think the Freedmen's Bureau played a crucial role in trying to establish some sense of order and justice in the post-war South, but it's also important to acknowledge the limitations and challenges they faced. @gilded-hip, what's your take on how the Bureau's efforts were impacted by the conflicting interests of Northern politicians and Southern landowners?"

logs.name: investor-enamel
logs.text: "@olive-offense, I agree that the Freedmen's Bureau faced significant challenges, but I think we should also discuss the role of the military in maintaining order during the Reconstruction era. @frigid-code, you mentioned the complex interests at play, but what are your thoughts on how the presence of federal troops impacted the balance of power in the South?"

logs.name: gilded-hip
logs.text: "@investor-enamel, that's a great point about the military's role in maintaining order, but let's not forget that their presence was also deeply resented by many Southerners who saw it as an occupation. @olive-offense, I think you're right that the Freedmen's Bureau faced significant challenges, but I'm not convinced that their efforts were entirely altruistic - what about the role of economic interests in shaping their policies? And @frigid-code, I'm still waiting to hear more about how you think the Reconstruction era laid the groundwork for the social and cultural changes that followed..."

logs.name: olive-offense
logs.text: "@gilded-hip, oh come on, you're not going to start with that whole 'the North was just trying to oppress the South' narrative again, are you? I mean, I get that the occupation wasn't popular, but let's be real, the Freedmen's Bureau was trying to help newly freed slaves, not exploit them. And as for economic interests, of course they played a role, but that doesn't mean the Bureau's efforts were entirely self-serving. @investor-enamel, what's your take on how the Bureau's focus on education and land ownership impacted the long-term prospects of African Americans in the South?"

logs.name: juvenile-duck
logs.text: "@olive-offense, spare us the drama, dear. The North was trying to oppress the South, and you know it. Don't act like the Freedmen's Bureau was just a bunch of saints. And @gilded-hip, I'm with you on this one - the occupation was a total disaster. @investor-enamel, what's your opinion on the carpetbaggers who came in and exploited the South during Reconstruction?"
